title: Nichrome Resistance Heating Wire Power Calculation
content: Nichrome (NiCr 80/20) resistance wire converts electrical energy to heat via Joule heating: P = V²/R = I²R. Resistivity ρ ≈ 1.10 × 10⁻⁶ Ω·m at 20 °C; resistance R = ρ·L / A, where L is wire length and A cross-sectional area.
extended_content: Nichrome 80/20 withstands continuous temperatures up to 1200 °C without significant oxidation. For hot-wire foam cutters: wire temperature 200–400 °C is sufficient to melt EPS/EPP foam cleanly. Practical design: 28–32 AWG nichrome wire, 12–24 V supply, 0.5–2 A current gives a wire temperature of 250–450 °C depending on tension and air exposure. Wire resistance drifts +10 % from 20 °C to 300 °C (positive temperature coefficient); this self-limiting effect provides moderate thermal stability. Tensioning the wire (using a spring or weight) maintains straightness as it expands thermally. Safety: nichrome at operating temperature can ignite flammable materials on contact — always de-energise before touching.
formula: P = V² / R = I² × R;  R = ρ × L / (π × (d/2)²)
units: P: W, R: Ω, L: m, d: m
source: Hoskins Manufacturing NiCr 80/20 Technical Data Sheet; Joule's Law
